agent_output: {"executive_summary":"This system exhibits five distinct architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over three years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy), the synchronous Notification fan-in (creating a system-wide availability bottleneck), and the Reporting Service's direct database reads (creating invisible coupling that will break under schema evolution). The god endpoint and HTTP-based background jobs are lower severity but still create operational and security risks. Remediation should be phased over three quarters, prioritizing the shared database and notification bottleneck first, as these pose the highest risk of cascading failures.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle management","boundaries":"VIOLATED — shares database with Courier Service, has its tables read directly by Reporting Service"},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier dispatch and tracking","boundaries":"VIOLATED — shares database with Order Service, meaning neither service owns its data"},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Send notifications across channels","boundaries":"VIOLATED — has become a synchronous dependency for 5 services, making it a single point of failure"},{"name":"Customer Service","responsibility":"Customer data management","boundaries":"VIOLATED — god endpoint exposes entire aggregate including payment methods, leaking bounded context internals"},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment processing","boundaries":"Partially intact but coupled to Notification Service synchronously"},{"name":"Reporting Service","responsibility":"Analytics and reporting","boundaries":"VIOLATED — bypasses Order Service API and reads directly from its database tables"},{"name":"Cron Job Scheduler","responsibility":"Trigger background processing","boundaries":"VIOLATED — uses HTTP calls to REST endpoints for job execution, conflating API concerns with job concerns"}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"Shared PostgreSQL database with schema prefixes","coupling_type":"database coupling (highest possible coupling)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP call","coupling_type":"temporal coupling"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP call","coupling_type":"temporal coupling"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP call","coupling_type":"temporal coupling"},{"from":"Customer Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP call","coupling_type":"temporal coupling"},{"from":"Reporting Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Synchronous HTTP call","coupling_type":"temporal coupling"},{"from":"Reporting Service","to":"Order Service DB","contract":"Direct SQL reads against Order tables","coupling_type":"database coupling (read-only but still structural)"},{"from":"Cron Scheduler","to":"Various Services","contract":"HTTP GET/POST to /api/v1/jobs/* endpoints","coupling_type":"inappropriate interface coupling"}],"data_flow":"Orders and courier data flow through a shared PostgreSQL instance. Five services synchronously call Notification Service on every state change. Reporting Service reads Order data directly from the database, bypassing the Order Service API entirely. Background jobs are triggered by cron via HTTP, meaning job execution shares the same process, thread pool, and rate limits as user-facing API traffic.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Shared Database (anti-pattern)","rationale":"Likely chosen for expediency when Courier was split from Order Service — avoided needing an integration layer"},{"pattern":"Synchronous Fan-In (anti-pattern)","rationale":"Notification was likely the first cross-cutting service, and synchronous HTTP was the simplest integration"},{"pattern":"God Endpoint (anti-pattern)","rationale":"Probably started as a convenience for a frontend that needed everything, then became the only endpoint"},{"pattern":"API-as-Job-Runner (anti-pattern)","rationale":"Avoided introducing a job framework by reusing existing HTTP infrastructure"},{"pattern":"Direct Database Read (anti-pattern)","rationale":"Reporting needed fast access to order data and the API was too slow or lacked needed queries"}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order","OrderLine","Refund"]},{"name":"Courier & Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["Courier","Delivery","Route"]},{"name":"Customer Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Customer","Address","Preferences"]},{"name":"Payment","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["PaymentMethod","Transaction","Refund"]},{"name":"Notification","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationTemplate","NotificationLog","Channel"]},{"name":"Reporting & Analytics","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Report","Dashboard","AnalyticsEvent"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier & Delivery","relationship":"CURRENT: Shared Kernel via database. TARGET: Customer-Supplier with domain events"},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Reporting & Analytics","relationship":"CURRENT: Direct DB read (Conformist to schema). TARGET: Published Language via CDC or event stream"},{"upstream":"All Services","downstream":"Notification","relationship":"CURRENT: Synchronous calls (each service is upstream). TARGET: Open Host Service consuming domain events asynchronously"},{"upstream":"Customer Management","downstream":"All Consumers","relationship":"CURRENT: God endpoint (Conformist). TARGET: Open Host Service with purpose-specific endpoints"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Split shared database between Order and Courier services","gains":"Independent deployability, independent schema evolution, failure isolation, clear data ownership","sacrifices":"Requires data synchronization mechanism, potential eventual consistency, migration complexity and risk","alternatives_considered":["Keep shared DB but enforce strict schema ownership rules via linting — lower effort but doesn't solve the coupling","Use database views as a contract layer — moderate effort but still couples at the infrastructure level"]},{"decision":"Replace synchronous notification calls with async event-driven pattern","gains":"Eliminates single point of failure, services no longer block on notification delivery, natural retry/dead-letter semantics","sacrifices":"Adds message broker infrastructure (operational complexity), eventual delivery means notifications may lag slightly, need to handle idempotency","alternatives_considered":["Add circuit breakers to synchronous calls — reduces cascading failure but doesn't fix the fundamental coupling","Fire-and-forget HTTP — loses delivery guarantees"]},{"decision":"Replace god endpoint with purpose-specific APIs","gains":"Reduced payload size, better cacheability, fine-grained authorization, follows Interface Segregation Principle","sacrifices":"More endpoints to maintain, clients must update, need API versioning strategy","alternatives_considered":["GraphQL — lets clients select fields but adds query complexity and potential N+1 issues","BFF (Backend for Frontend) — good if there are multiple client types but adds a layer"]},{"decision":"Move background jobs to a proper job runner","gains":"Separation of job and API concerns, independent scaling, proper retry/backoff, no shared thread pool with API traffic","sacrifices":"New infrastructure component, migration of existing jobs, new monitoring needs","alternatives_considered":["Keep HTTP endpoints but add a separate internal-only port — partial fix, still conflates concerns","Serverless functions (Lambda/Cloud Functions) — good for stateless jobs but adds cold start latency"]},{"decision":"Introduce CDC or event stream for Reporting","gains":"Reporting gets near-real-time data without coupling to Order schema, Order Service can evolve freely","sacrifices":"CDC infrastructure (Debezium/equivalent), potential lag, schema registry needed","alternatives_considered":["Materialized views in a separate read replica — simpler but still couples at schema level","Order Service publishes to a data warehouse on write — adds write-path complexity to Order Service"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification delivery mechanism","evolution_path":"Once event-driven, can easily add new notification channels (SMS, push, Slack) as new consumers without modifying publishers"},{"area":"Reporting data pipeline","evolution_path":"CDC stream can feed not just reporting but also ML pipelines, search indexes, and data lake without additional coupling"},{"area":"Customer API surface","evolution_path":"Purpose-specific endpoints enable per-consumer authorization, rate limiting, and caching strategies"},{"area":"Background job framework","evolution_path":"Proper job runner enables scheduled jobs, one-off jobs, priority queues, and job chaining"}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order-Courier database split migration","reason":"Data migration must be done carefully with dual-write period. Schema entanglement after 3 years means foreign keys, shared sequences, and possibly shared stored procedures must be untangled."},{"area":"Notification contract","reason":"Five services must all be updated to publish events instead of making synchronous calls. Requires coordinated rollout or a transitional adapter layer."},{"area":"Client contracts for god endpoint","reason":"Unknown number of consumers depend on the /everything shape. Breaking this requires discovery of all clients and coordinated migration."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Shared database allows Courier Service schema changes to break Order Service (and vice versa) with no compile-time safety net","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Phase 1: Immediately add database-level schema ownership enforcement (separate PostgreSQL schemas with restricted grants). Phase 2: Split into separate databases with event-based sync."},{"risk":"Notification Service downtime cascades to all five dependent services, halting order placement, payment processing, and courier dispatch","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Immediate: Add circuit breakers with fallback (queue-and-retry). Medium-term: Move to async event-driven notifications."},{"risk":"Reporting Service breaks silently when Order Service evolves its schema since there is no contract between them","severity":"high","mitigation":"Short-term: Add integration tests that verify Reporting queries against Order schema. Medium-term: CDC stream with schema registry."},{"risk":"God endpoint leaks payment methods and sensitive data to any consumer that calls it, violating principle of least privilege","severity":"high","mitigation":"Immediate: Audit who calls this endpoint and what fields they use. Add field-level filtering. Medium-term: Split into purpose-specific endpoints."},{"risk":"Background jobs running as API endpoints share thread pools and memory with user-facing traffic — a long-running refund job can starve API requests","severity":"high","mitigation":"Immediate: Run jobs on separate worker instances hitting the same endpoints. Medium-term: Migrate to dedicated job runner."},{"risk":"Cron-triggered HTTP jobs have no built-in retry, dead-letter, or distributed locking — concurrent cron executions can cause duplicate refund processing","severity":"high","mitigation":"Immediate: Add distributed locks (Redis/advisory locks). Medium-term: Proper job framework with idempotency keys."},{"risk":"Migration effort is substantial and could destabilize production if attempted as a big-bang rewrite","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Phased approach with strangler fig pattern. Each anti-pattern remediated independently with rollback capability."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Three-phase remediation over 3 quarters. Phase 1 (Q1 — Stabilize): Add circuit breakers to Notification calls, enforce database schema ownership via PostgreSQL grants, add distributed locks to cron jobs, audit god endpoint consumers. Phase 2 (Q2 — Decouple): Introduce message broker (RabbitMQ/Kafka depending on scale), migrate Notification to event-driven, split Order/Courier databases using dual-write then cutover, introduce CDC for Reporting. Phase 3 (Q3 — Refine): Split god endpoint into purpose-specific APIs, migrate background jobs to proper job runner (Sidekiq/Temporal/Bull depending on stack), decommission direct database reads.","files_to_create_or_modify":["infrastructure/message-broker/ — broker configuration and topic definitions","order-service/events/ — domain event publishers for order state changes","courier-service/events/ — domain event publishers and consumers","notification-service/consumers/ — event consumers replacing synchronous API handlers","customer-service/api/v2/ — purpose-specific endpoints (profile, payment-methods, order-history, addresses, preferences)","reporting-service/consumers/ — CDC stream consumers replacing direct DB reads","shared/circuit-breaker/ — circuit breaker configuration for transitional period","jobs/ — dedicated job runner service with proper retry, dead-letter, and idempotency"],"patterns_to_follow":"Strangler Fig for incremental migration. Anti-Corruption Layer between old and new integration patterns during transition. Event Carried State Transfer for cross-service data needs. Outbox Pattern to ensure reliable event publishing from database transactions. Idempotent Consumers for all event handlers.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Big-bang migration — never try to fix all five anti-patterns simultaneously","Introducing a message broker without idempotency — will trade database coupling for message-processing bugs","Splitting the database without understanding all cross-schema queries first — run query analysis on pg_stat_statements before planning the split","Adding events while keeping synchronous calls — dual paths create confusion about source of truth","Assuming the god endpoint has few consumers — it was probably convenient so it likely has many undocumented consumers"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Anti-pattern identification — these are textbook architectural smells with well-understood remediation paths","Prioritization — shared database and notification fan-in are objectively the highest-risk items","Remediation patterns — strangler fig, event-driven, CDC are proven approaches for these exact problems"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact technology choices (Kafka vs RabbitMQ, Temporal vs Sidekiq) — depends on team expertise, scale, and existing infrastructure","Migration timeline — 3 quarters is an estimate; actual effort depends on schema entanglement depth and team capacity","God endpoint consumer inventory — cannot assess blast radius without auditing actual traffic"],"assumptions":["Team has capacity to operate a message broker","PostgreSQL is the only shared data store (no shared Redis, S3, etc.)","Services are deployed independently (containerized or similar)","There is no service mesh or API gateway already handling circuit breaking"]}}
